learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Greedy Algorithm, Loops, Dynamic Programming Basics, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine crossing stepping stones across a river. Every stone tells you how far you can jump from it. Instead of making the longest jump every time, your goal is to reach the last stone using the fewest jumps possible.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine crossing stepping stones across a river. Every stone tells you how far you can jump from it. Instead of making the longest jump every time, your goal is to reach the last stone using the fewest jumps possible.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Greedy BFS pattern whenever you need the minimum number of jumps or moves while each position expands the reachable range.
learning.signals: Whenever you need the minimum number of moves while each position expands the reachable range, think of Greedy BFS Level Traversal.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given a 0-indexed integer array nums where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. Starting from the first index, return the minimum number of jumps required to reach the last index. You can assume that you can always reach the last index.

examples.explanation: Jump from index 0 to index 1, then from index 1 to index 4.
The minimum number of jumps is 2.

constraints: It is guaranteed that the last index is reachable.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Try every possible jump recursively and return the minimum jumps required.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Think of each jump as covering a range of indices. While exploring one range, keep track of the farthest position reachable. Once the current range ends, make one jump and move to the next range. This is similar to performing BFS level by level.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Each jump explores one reachable range just like one level of BFS.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever you need the minimum number of moves while each position expands the reachable range, think of Greedy BFS Level Traversal.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Every index is processed exactly once. The current boundary represents one BFS level, while the farthest reachable position defines the next level. This guarantees the minimum number of jumps.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever you need the minimum number of moves while each position expands the reachable range, think of Greedy BFS Level Traversal.
commonWrongPaths: Traversing until n instead of n-1.
Confusing Jump Game I with Jump Game II.

hints: Treat every jump as one BFS level.
Increase jumps only when the current range ends.
commonMisconceptions: Traversing until n instead of n-1.
Confusing Jump Game I with Jump Game II.

pattern.name: Greedy BFS Level Traversal
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever you need the minimum number of moves while each position expands the reachable range, think of Greedy BFS Level Traversal.

approaches.title: Recursive Backtracking
approaches.idea: Try every possible jump recursively and return the minimum jumps required.

code.java: Recursive DFS solution omitted because it causes exponential complexity.

approaches.title: Dynamic Programming
approaches.idea: Use DP where dp[i] stores the minimum jumps needed to reach index i.
approaches.dryRun: Fill DP array from left to right.

approaches.title: Greedy BFS Level Traversal
approaches.idea: Maintain the current jump boundary and the farthest index reachable within that boundary. Traverse the array while updating the farthest reachable position. Whenever the current index reaches the boundary, increment the jump count and expand the boundary to the farthest reachable index.
approaches.intuition: Every index is processed exactly once. The current boundary represents one BFS level, while the farthest reachable position defines the next level. This guarantees the minimum number of jumps.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[2,3,1,1,4]
Jump=0 End=0
Reach=2
Boundary reached → Jump=1 End=2
Reach=4
Boundary reached → Jump=2
Answer=2

visualization.description: Each jump covers one reachable range.

interviewFollowUps: Why is this equivalent to BFS?
How does it differ from Jump Game I?
Can you reconstruct the actual path?
What if the destination is unreachable?
interviewTips: Explain why each boundary represents one jump.
Relate the greedy solution to BFS levels.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Each jump explores one reachable range just like one level of BFS.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Track Boundary → Expand Farthest → Jump at Boundary.

commonMistakes: Traversing until n instead of n-1.
Confusing Jump Game I with Jump Game II.
